mnemonics.title: Zero Is The Hero
mnemonics.content: Array indices ALWAYS start at 0, not 1. Remember: First element is at index 0, second at index 1, and so on. For an array of size 5, valid indices are 0,1,2,3,4.

mnemonics.title: Please Excuse My Dear Aunt Sally
mnemonics.content: Operator Precedence: Parentheses → Exponents (not in C++) → Multiplication/Division → Addition/Subtraction. In C++: () → * / % → + - → =

mnemonics.title: Prefix Postfix Puzzle
mnemonics.content: ++x (prefix) increments THEN uses the value; x++ (postfix) uses the value THEN increments. Think: 'First increment, then assign' vs 'First assign, then increment'.

mnemonics.title: Row-Major Rules
mnemonics.content: In 2D arrays, elements are stored row by row (contiguously in memory). Access: arr[row][col]. Visualize walking through each complete row before moving to the next.

mnemonics.title: Logical Short-Circuit
mnemonics.content: AND (&&): If first condition is FALSE, skip the rest (result is false). OR (||): If first condition is TRUE, skip the rest (result is true). Short-circuit saves time!

acronyms.expansion: Array subscript notation - accessing element at index i
acronyms.usage: Use to read or modify the value stored at position i in the array. Always ensure i is within bounds (0 to size-1).

acronyms.expansion: Size of entire array in bytes
acronyms.usage: Use to find total bytes. To find number of elements: sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0])

acronyms.expansion: Constant integer - fixed value that cannot be changed
acronyms.usage: Use for array sizes to avoid 'magic numbers' in code. Improves readability and prevents accidental changes.
Use as a compact if-else for simple conditions. Example: max = (a > b) ? a : b;
Use ++i when you need the incremented value immediately; use i++ when you need the original value first, then increment happens after.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a single row of numbered mailboxes or lockers. Each locker has a unique index number (starting from 0) and can store one value. To find a specific locker, count from the beginning - the first one is number 0!

Picture a classroom seating arrangement or a chessboard. Rows and columns define each seat. arr[2][3] means row 2, column 3. Think of it as 'row first, column second' - like reading a book page by page.
Visualize a long continuous ribbon of connected boxes in computer memory. Each box is adjacent to the next - no gaps! This is why arrays provide fast (O(1)) random access.
Draw an inverted tree for complex expressions. The root is the final operation, branches are sub-expressions. Evaluate from bottom to top. This helps visualize operator precedence clearly.
Think of array name as a starting address on a street. Adding 1 moves to the next house (next element), not next byte. The compiler handles the actual byte offset based on data type size.

analogies.analogy: Like reserving a block of seats in a theater. You tell the booking clerk how many seats you need (size), and they give you consecutive seats. You can't change the number of seats later (static array).

Like trying to enter a seat number that doesn't exist in the theater. Seat 15 when the row only has seats 1-10. The booking system (program) will crash or behave unexpectedly - always check seat numbers first!

Think of solving a math problem following BODMAS/PEMDAS rules. Just as you do multiplication before addition in math, C++ follows operator precedence strictly. Parentheses are like solving mini-problems first.
Like copying content from one notebook to another. The right side (source) gets evaluated first, then copied to the left side (destination). It's not equality like in math - it's assignment!
Like needing BOTH a library card AND a valid ID to borrow books. If you don't have one, you can't borrow - the second condition doesn't matter. This is short-circuit evaluation.
Like having either a bus pass OR a metro card to travel. If you have one, you can travel - no need to check the other. This is short-circuit evaluation.
Think of a multiplication table or Excel spreadsheet. Rows represent one category (like days), columns represent another (like time slots). Each cell (intersection) holds a value.
